summary: Gruber systematically dismantles AccuWeather's official response to revelations that their iOS app was sending Wi-Fi router names and MAC addresses to data monetization firm Reveal Mobile, even when users opted out of location tracking. He identifies multiple deceptive tactics in the statement: deflecting to GPS coordinates when the issue was Wi-Fi data, claiming router info isn't 'user information,' and shifting blame by saying the data was 'unused by AccuWeather' when Reveal Mobile was the one exploiting it. Gruber highlights that AccuWeather's claim of being unaware what the Reveal SDK did is itself a damning admission of negligence. He argues the company's invocation of 'evolving best practices' is really about what they can get away with before being caught. The piece concludes by translating AccuWeather's corporate gratitude toward their 'supportive community' as a thinly veiled dismissal of the security researcher who exposed them.
key_points: AccuWeather's denial focused on GPS coordinates, but the actual accusation involved Wi-Fi router names and MAC addresses that can locate users within meters
Calling Wi-Fi router data 'not user information' and saying it was 'unused by AccuWeather' are sleight-of-hand deflections, since Reveal Mobile was the entity monetizing the location data
AccuWeather's claim of being unaware what the embedded Reveal SDK did is not a defense but a shocking admission of negligence
The 'evolving best practices' framing conflates privacy standards with what companies can get away with before being caught
Reveal Mobile's own marketing materials describe their business as selling consumer location data to retailers, undermining their claim that no reverse engineering of locations occurred
money_quotes: Claiming this is about GPS coordinates is like if they were caught stealing debit cards and they issued a denial that they never stole anyone's cash.
If true, AccuWeather is seemingly claiming they embedded Reveal Mobile's SDK in their app without knowing what it did. I believe them. But that's a shocking admission of negligence.
The best practices for respecting the privacy of users do not change from day to day.

What they mean is that one day your app can be doing something shady unbeknownst to the world, and the next day it can be discovered and widely publicized, painting your company as untrustworthy. But that's not about 'best practices' — that's about what you can get away with changing from one day to the next.
Why else would they be collecting router MAC addresses if not to use a reverse lookup to locate users?
key_insight: AccuWeather's corporate response to being caught sending user location data to a monetization firm is itself a masterclass in deceptive PR, with every sentence carefully constructed to deflect rather than address the actual accusation.
